name: Messier 60 Black Hole
Overview:
The Messier 60 Black Hole is a massive supermassive black hole located at the center of Messier 60 (M60), one of the largest elliptical galaxies in the Virgo Cluster. With an estimated mass of approximately 4.5 billion times that of the Sun, it ranks among the larger black holes in the nearby universe and plays a crucial role in the evolution of its host galaxy.
Messier 60 is one of the brightest galaxies within the Virgo Cluster and contains trillions of stars spread across a vast elliptical structure. The galaxy serves as the dominant member of a small galaxy group and exerts a strong gravitational influence on nearby galaxies. At its center lies a dormant but extremely massive black hole that was once far more active than it appears today.
Although the black hole is currently inactive, evidence suggests it experienced energetic active phases in the past. Observations reveal large cavities and radio structures that indicate powerful jets were once launched from the region surrounding the black hole, shaping the galaxy's environment over millions of years.
Discovery & History:
Messier 60 was discovered in April 1779 by German astronomer Johann Gottfried Koehler while observing a comet passing through the Virgo constellation. Just a few days later, Charles Messier independently recorded the object and added it to his famous Messier Catalogue as M60.
For centuries, M60 was known simply as one of the brightest elliptical galaxies visible from Earth. As astronomical technology improved, researchers began studying its structure, stellar population, and unusual interactions with nearby galaxies.
The presence of a central supermassive black hole was confirmed through measurements of stellar motions near the galactic core. By analyzing how stars orbit around the center, astronomers determined that an enormous concentration of invisible mass must exist there.
Modern observations have since revealed that the black hole has influenced the galaxy for billions of years and likely underwent periods of intense activity during its evolutionary history.
Physical Characteristics:
The black hole at the center of Messier 60 has an estimated mass of approximately 4.5 billion solar masses. This makes it slightly larger than Sagittarius A* by more than a thousand times and places it among the most massive black holes in the local universe.
The host galaxy itself contains roughly one trillion solar masses of material, including stars, gas, and dark matter. Nearly half of this mass may consist of dark matter distributed throughout the galaxy's enormous halo.
Unlike active quasars that continuously consume large amounts of matter, the black hole in M60 is relatively quiet today. However, X-ray observations reveal evidence that it was once much more energetic and capable of launching powerful jets into intergalactic space.
The galaxy also contains a large reservoir of hot gas that emits strongly in X-rays, allowing astronomers to study interactions between the black hole and its surrounding environment.
Scientific Importance:
Messier 60 is an important laboratory for understanding the evolution of giant elliptical galaxies and their central black holes. Because the galaxy is relatively close to Earth, astronomers can study its structure in greater detail than many more distant systems.
The black hole provides valuable insight into the relationship between galaxy mass and black hole growth. By comparing M60 to other giant elliptical galaxies such as M87 and NGC 4889, researchers can investigate how supermassive black holes influence the development of large galaxies.
The system is also important for understanding active galactic nuclei. Evidence suggests that the black hole was once significantly more active and produced energetic outflows that altered the distribution of gas throughout the galaxy.
Studying these past interactions helps scientists understand how black holes regulate star formation and shape the long-term evolution of galaxies.
Observations & Modern Research:
X-ray observations have revealed cavities within the hot gas surrounding the galaxy. These cavities are believed to have been created by powerful jets emitted by the black hole during earlier active periods.
Astronomers have also studied M60 because of its relationship with neighboring galaxies. One notable companion is NGC 4647, a spiral galaxy that appears extremely close to M60 in the sky. Evidence suggests that tidal interactions between the two galaxies may have recently begun.
In 2013, researchers discovered M60-UCD1, one of the densest known galaxies, orbiting near Messier 60. This ultra-compact dwarf galaxy has become an important object for studying galaxy evolution and black hole formation.
Modern observatories continue monitoring M60 across optical, radio, ultraviolet, and X-ray wavelengths to better understand the black hole's influence on its environment and reconstruct its active past.
Quick Stats:
Type: Supermassive Black Hole
Estimated Mass: ~4.5 Billion Solar Masses
Host Galaxy: Messier 60 (NGC 4649)
Galaxy Cluster: Virgo Cluster
Distance from Earth: ~57 Million Light-Years
Constellation: Virgo
Galaxy Type: Giant Elliptical Galaxy
Discovery of Galaxy: 1779
Current Activity: Largely Inactive
Evidence of Past Activity: X-ray Cavities and Radio Lobes
Scientific Significance: Black Hole Feedback in Giant Elliptical Galaxies
Nearby Companion: NGC 4647
Notable Satellite: M60-UCD1

name: Cygnus X-1
Overview:
Cygnus X-1 is one of the most famous black holes in the history of astronomy and is widely recognized as the first black hole candidate to gain broad scientific acceptance. Located within the constellation Cygnus, it is part of a powerful binary star system consisting of a massive blue supergiant star and a stellar-mass black hole.
Unlike the supermassive black holes found at the centers of galaxies, Cygnus X-1 belongs to a class known as stellar black holes, which form from the collapse of massive stars. Despite being much smaller than the giant black holes found in quasars and galaxy cores, Cygnus X-1 played a crucial role in proving that black holes truly exist.
The system emits enormous amounts of X-rays, making it one of the brightest X-ray sources visible from Earth. These X-rays are produced when gas from the companion star falls toward the black hole and heats to millions of degrees before crossing the event horizon.
Because of its proximity and brightness, Cygnus X-1 remains one of the most studied black holes ever discovered and continues to serve as a key laboratory for understanding gravity, accretion disks, jets, and black hole physics.
Discovery & History:
Cygnus X-1 was first discovered in 1964 during a rocket-based X-ray survey of the sky. Scientists detected an unusually powerful source of X-rays coming from the direction of the Cygnus constellation.
For several years, astronomers attempted to determine the nature of the object responsible for these emissions. In 1972, observations revealed that the X-ray source was associated with a massive blue supergiant star called HDE 226868. Measurements of the star's motion indicated that it was orbiting an invisible companion with a mass too large to be a neutron star.
This evidence strongly suggested the presence of a black hole, making Cygnus X-1 one of the earliest and strongest black hole candidates ever identified.
The object became even more famous because of a scientific wager between physicists Stephen Hawking and Kip Thorne. Hawking initially bet against Cygnus X-1 being a black hole, but later conceded after overwhelming observational evidence confirmed its nature.
Physical Characteristics:
Current measurements estimate the black hole's mass to be approximately 21 solar masses, making it one of the most massive stellar black holes known within the Milky Way.
The black hole orbits a blue supergiant companion star that contains roughly 30 solar masses. Together they form a high-mass X-ray binary system, completing one orbit every 5.6 days.
Material from the companion star is continuously pulled toward the black hole through powerful stellar winds. This gas forms a rapidly rotating accretion disk around the black hole where temperatures reach millions of degrees.
The black hole's event horizon is estimated to be only a few dozen kilometers across, yet it generates more energy than entire star systems through the process of accretion.
Studies suggest the black hole spins extremely rapidly, approaching the maximum rotation speed allowed by the laws of physics.
Accretion Disk & X-Ray Emissions:
One of Cygnus X-1's most remarkable features is its extremely bright X-ray emission.
As gas spirals inward toward the black hole, friction within the accretion disk heats the material to temperatures of millions of degrees. This produces intense X-rays that can be detected across vast distances.
The system frequently switches between different emission states known as the "hard state" and the "soft state." These changes provide valuable information about how matter behaves near black holes.
Observations have revealed rapid fluctuations in brightness occurring within milliseconds, indicating that the emission originates from a very compact region close to the event horizon.
These observations helped establish the modern understanding of how black holes consume matter and generate high-energy radiation.
Relativistic Jets:
Cygnus X-1 also produces powerful relativistic jets.
These jets emerge from regions near the black hole and travel through space at a significant fraction of the speed of light. They carry enormous amounts of energy away from the system and can extend for vast distances into interstellar space.
One of the jets has created a large shockwave structure as it collides with surrounding gas clouds. This interaction produces glowing nebula-like structures that can be observed in radio and optical wavelengths.
The existence of these jets demonstrates that black holes are not merely cosmic vacuum cleaners. Instead, they are capable of influencing and reshaping their environments on a large scale.
Cygnus X-1 serves as an important example of how stellar black holes inject energy into the Milky Way.
Scientific Importance:
Cygnus X-1 occupies a unique place in the history of astrophysics.
It provided some of the first convincing evidence that black holes are real astronomical objects rather than purely theoretical predictions.
The system has allowed scientists to test theories of General Relativity, accretion physics, jet formation, and black hole evolution. Because it is relatively close to Earth compared to many other black holes, it remains one of the best observational laboratories available.
Researchers continue using Cygnus X-1 to study how black holes form from massive stars, how they grow through accretion, and how they affect surrounding matter.
The object also serves as a bridge between stellar black holes and the much larger supermassive black holes found in distant galaxies.
Modern Research:
Modern observatories including Chandra, Hubble, NICER, and numerous radio telescopes continue to monitor Cygnus X-1.
Recent measurements have refined the black hole's mass and confirmed that it spins at an extremely high rate. Researchers are also studying the structure of its accretion disk, the behavior of its jets, and the interaction between the black hole and its companion star.
Evidence suggests the black hole may have formed through a direct collapse of a massive star rather than a traditional supernova explosion. This discovery has important implications for understanding black hole formation throughout the universe.
Cygnus X-1 remains one of the most observed and scientifically valuable black holes known today.
Quick Stats:
Type: Stellar Black Hole
Estimated Mass: ~21 Solar Masses
Host System: Cygnus X-1 Binary System
Companion Star: HDE 226868
Distance from Earth: ~7,200 Light-Years
Constellation: Cygnus
Discovery Year: 1964
Orbital Period: 5.6 Days
Black Hole Category: High-Mass Stellar Black Hole
Special Feature: First Widely Accepted Black Hole Candidate
Jet Presence: Yes
Scientific Importance: Helped Confirm the Existence of Black Holes

name: M87*
Overview:
M87* is a supermassive black hole located at the center of the giant elliptical galaxy Messier 87 (M87), one of the largest galaxies in the nearby universe. With a mass of approximately 6.5 billion times that of the Sun, M87* is among the most massive black holes ever directly observed. It resides within a galaxy that contains trillions of stars and dominates the gravitational environment of its host system.
M87* became one of the most famous objects in astronomy in April 2019 when scientists released the first direct image of a black hole. The image, captured by the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT), revealed a glowing ring of superheated gas surrounding a dark central shadow. This historic achievement provided visual confirmation of decades of theoretical predictions regarding black holes and Einstein's General Theory of Relativity.
Unlike Sagittarius A*, which lies at the center of our own galaxy, M87* is located in a distant galaxy within the Virgo Cluster. Despite being much farther away, its enormous size made it easier to image than many closer black holes. Today, M87* remains one of the most important laboratories for studying extreme gravity, black hole physics, and energetic cosmic phenomena.
Discovery & History:
The galaxy Messier 87 was first discovered by French astronomer Charles Messier in 1781 and was originally cataloged as a nebula. For many years astronomers could not determine its true nature, but later observations revealed it to be a massive galaxy located far beyond the Milky Way.
During the early twentieth century, astronomers noticed a strange jet of material extending outward from the center of M87. This unusual feature suggested that an extremely energetic object was hidden within the galaxy's core. As observational technology improved, evidence accumulated that the galaxy hosted a supermassive black hole.
Over several decades, researchers used stellar motions, gas dynamics, radio observations, X-ray measurements, and advanced computer models to estimate the black hole's mass. By the late twentieth century, M87* had become one of the strongest candidates for a supermassive black hole. This conclusion was spectacularly confirmed in 2019 when the Event Horizon Telescope produced the first image of its shadow.
Physical Characteristics:
M87* possesses an estimated mass of approximately 6.5 billion solar masses, making it more than 1,500 times heavier than Sagittarius A*, the black hole at the center of the Milky Way. Its immense gravity controls the movement of stars, gas clouds, and matter near the galactic core.
The black hole is surrounded by a rotating accretion disk composed of extremely hot gas. Matter spiraling toward the event horizon is accelerated to tremendous speeds and heated to extraordinary temperatures, causing it to emit large amounts of radiation. This glowing material formed the bright ring visible in the famous Event Horizon Telescope image.
Scientists estimate that M87* rotates rapidly and continuously consumes matter from its surroundings. The black hole's event horizon is so large that its diameter exceeds the size of our Solar System. Its gravitational influence extends far beyond the immediate vicinity of the black hole itself, affecting the structure and evolution of the host galaxy.
Scientific Importance:
M87* is one of the most important astronomical objects ever studied because it provided the first direct image of a black hole. Before 2019, black holes had only been inferred through indirect evidence such as stellar motion, radiation, and gravitational effects. The image of M87* offered direct observational confirmation of the existence of black holes and validated key predictions made by General Relativity.
The black hole also serves as an important laboratory for understanding how supermassive black holes interact with their host galaxies. By studying M87*, scientists can investigate the relationship between black hole growth, galaxy evolution, and the behavior of matter under extreme gravitational conditions.
Research on M87* continues to improve our understanding of gravity, spacetime, magnetic fields, and the energetic processes occurring near event horizons. It remains one of the most valuable objects for testing modern theories of astrophysics.
Observations & Modern Research:
One of the most remarkable features of M87* is its enormous relativistic jet. This jet consists of highly energetic particles that are launched from the region surrounding the black hole and extend thousands of light-years into space. The jet travels at speeds close to that of light and is powered by interactions between the black hole's rotation, magnetic fields, and accreting matter.
In April 2019, the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration released the first direct image of the black hole's shadow. This achievement required a global network of radio telescopes operating together as a virtual Earth-sized observatory. The image immediately became one of the most iconic scientific photographs ever produced.
In 2021, researchers revealed polarized images of M87*, providing new insights into the magnetic fields surrounding the black hole. These observations helped explain how the powerful jet is generated and how energy is transported away from the black hole. More recently, improved image reconstruction techniques have produced sharper views of the black hole and its environment.
Today, M87* continues to be monitored by observatories across multiple wavelengths, including radio, infrared, optical, X-ray, and gamma-ray telescopes. Future observations are expected to reveal even more details about the extreme physics occurring near its event horizon.
Quick Stats:
Type: Supermassive Black Hole
Mass: ~6.5 Billion Solar Masses
Distance from Earth: ~53 Million Light-Years
Host Galaxy: Messier 87 (M87)
Constellation: Virgo
First Direct Image Released: 2019
Observed By: Event Horizon Telescope
Notable Feature: First Black Hole Ever Imaged
Jet Length: Over 5,000 Light-Years
Significance: First Direct Visual Evidence of a Black Hole

name: TON 618
Location: Near the Border of the Constellations Canes Venatici and Coma Berenices
Overview:
TON 618 is one of the most extreme and powerful objects ever discovered in the known universe. It is a hyperluminous quasar powered by an ultramassive black hole at its center. Quasars are among the brightest objects in existence, formed when enormous amounts of matter fall into a supermassive black hole and release vast quantities of energy. TON 618 shines with such intensity that it outshines its entire host galaxy, making the galaxy itself nearly impossible to observe from Earth.
What makes TON 618 truly remarkable is the incredible mass of its central black hole. Current estimates place its mass at approximately 40.7 billion times that of the Sun, while older estimates suggested a mass as high as 66 billion solar masses. This places TON 618 among the most massive black holes ever identified and has led some astronomers to classify it as an "ultramassive" black hole.
The light we see from TON 618 today began its journey when the universe was much younger. Because of its immense distance, observing TON 618 allows astronomers to study conditions that existed billions of years in the past, providing valuable information about the early universe and the formation of massive galaxies.
Discovery & History:
TON 618 was first cataloged in 1957 during a survey of faint blue stars conducted at the Tonantzintla Observatory in Mexico. At the time, astronomers did not yet understand the nature of quasars, so the object was simply recorded as a faint blue stellar source. It received the designation "618" in the Tonantzintla Catalogue, which later became the name by which it is known today.
The true nature of TON 618 remained unknown until 1970, when radio observations revealed that the object emitted powerful radio waves. Further spectroscopic studies showed emission lines characteristic of quasars and indicated that the object was located at an enormous distance from Earth. These findings established TON 618 as one of the most luminous quasars known at the time.
As astronomical instruments improved, researchers continued to investigate TON 618 and discovered that its extraordinary brightness was powered by an exceptionally massive black hole. Over the decades, it became one of the most important objects for studying the upper limits of black hole growth and the evolution of quasars in the early universe.
Physical Characteristics:
The central black hole of TON 618 is estimated to have a mass of approximately 40.7 billion Suns, making it one of the largest black holes ever measured. Older calculations suggested an even greater mass of around 66 billion solar masses. To put this into perspective, the black hole is more than 9,000 times more massive than Sagittarius A*, the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way.
TON 618 is classified as a hyperluminous quasar because of the enormous amount of energy released by matter falling into its accretion disk. The object radiates approximately 140 trillion times more energy than the Sun, making it one of the brightest known objects in the universe. The surrounding gas moves at extremely high velocities under the influence of the black hole's gravity, producing unusually broad spectral emission lines.
The event horizon of TON 618 is enormous. Estimates suggest that its diameter would extend far beyond the orbit of Pluto if placed at the center of our Solar System. Depending on the mass estimate used, the event horizon could span hundreds of astronomical units, making it one of the largest black hole event horizons known.
Scientific Importance:
TON 618 represents one of the most extreme examples of black hole growth in the universe. Its enormous mass challenges existing theories about how black holes form and evolve. Understanding how a black hole could grow to tens of billions of solar masses helps astronomers improve models of galaxy formation and cosmic evolution.
The object is also important because it provides a glimpse into the early universe. Since its light has traveled for billions of years before reaching Earth, studying TON 618 allows scientists to investigate the conditions that existed when galaxies and quasars were still developing. Observations of objects like TON 618 help explain how the largest structures in the cosmos formed and changed over time.
Some researchers have suggested that TON 618 belongs to a proposed class of ultramassive black holes, objects whose masses exceed those of typical supermassive black holes by a significant margin. If confirmed, these objects may represent a distinct stage of black hole evolution.
Observations & Modern Research:
Modern observations have revealed that TON 618 is surrounded by an enormous cloud of hydrogen-rich gas known as a Lyman-alpha nebula. This nebula extends for roughly 330,000 light-years, making it about three times larger than the Milky Way. Such structures are among the largest known gaseous formations in the universe.
In 2021, observations from the Atacama Large Millimeter Array (ALMA) provided new insights into this giant nebula. Researchers found that it contains vast quantities of molecular gas and is strongly influenced by radiation and energetic outflows produced by the central quasar. The intense energy from TON 618 causes hydrogen throughout the nebula to glow brightly, making it visible across immense cosmic distances.
Scientists continue to study TON 618 to better understand black hole feeding processes, quasar activity, galaxy evolution, and the relationship between supermassive black holes and their surrounding environments. Because of its extraordinary size and luminosity, TON 618 remains one of the most valuable laboratories for exploring extreme astrophysical phenomena.
Quick Stats:
Type: Ultramassive Black Hole / Hyperluminous Quasar
Black Hole Mass: ~40.7 Billion Solar Masses
Older Mass Estimate: ~66 Billion Solar Masses
Distance from Earth: ~10.8 Billion Light-Years
Constellation: Canes Venatici / Coma Berenices
Luminosity: ~140 Trillion Times the Sun
Classification: Radio-Loud Quasar
Special Feature: One of the Largest Known Black Holes
Associated Structure: Giant Lyman-Alpha Nebula
Scientific Significance: Extreme Black Hole Growth and Early Universe Research

name: Phoenix A Black Hole
Location: Center of Phoenix A Galaxy, Phoenix Cluster
Overview:
The Phoenix A Black Hole is one of the most extreme and potentially largest black holes ever proposed in the observable universe. Located at the center of Phoenix A, the dominant galaxy of the Phoenix Cluster, this colossal object powers an active galactic nucleus that exhibits characteristics of both a quasar and a Seyfert galaxy. The immense energy released by the black hole influences not only its host galaxy but the entire surrounding galaxy cluster.
What makes the Phoenix A Black Hole extraordinary is its estimated mass. Current theoretical models suggest that it may contain around 100 billion times the mass of the Sun, potentially making it the most massive black hole known. While this estimate has not yet been directly confirmed through orbital measurements, the evidence gathered from the galaxy's behavior and energy output strongly suggests the presence of an incredibly massive central object.
The Phoenix Cluster itself is one of the most powerful galaxy clusters ever discovered. At its center lies Phoenix A, a giant elliptical galaxy that contains enormous amounts of hot gas and exhibits some of the highest rates of star formation ever observed in a galaxy cluster. The central black hole plays a crucial role in regulating these processes, making it one of the most important objects for understanding galaxy evolution.
Discovery & History:
The Phoenix Cluster was discovered through observations conducted by the South Pole Telescope survey and later studied extensively using multiple space- and ground-based observatories. Astronomers quickly realized that the cluster was unusual because of its extreme brightness in X-rays and the enormous quantities of hot gas concentrated in its center.
As researchers investigated the cluster further, they identified Phoenix A as its dominant central galaxy. Observations revealed that this galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus powered by a supermassive black hole. Unlike most galaxy clusters where black hole activity suppresses star formation, Phoenix A appeared to be producing stars at an unprecedented rate.
In 2016, researchers developed advanced evolutionary models to estimate the mass of the central black hole. These studies suggested that the black hole could possess a mass approaching 100 billion solar masses, placing it well beyond the range of typical supermassive black holes and potentially within a new category of cosmic giants.
More recently, observations from the James Webb Space Telescope have provided additional insights into the unusual processes occurring within the Phoenix Cluster, helping astronomers understand how such an extreme environment can exist.
Physical Characteristics:
The Phoenix A Black Hole is believed to possess a mass of roughly 100 billion solar masses. If confirmed, this would make it significantly larger than other famous black holes such as Sagittarius A*, M87*, and TON 618.
The estimated event horizon of the black hole is truly enormous. Scientists calculate that its Schwarzschild radius could extend approximately 2,000 astronomical units from the center. For comparison, Pluto orbits the Sun at an average distance of about 40 astronomical units. If placed at the center of our Solar System, the event horizon would extend far beyond the outer planets and reach deep into the inner Oort Cloud.
The circumference of the event horizon is so vast that even light would require more than 71 days to complete a single journey around it. This scale highlights just how enormous the object may be compared to most other known black holes.
The black hole also powers powerful jets and energetic outflows that interact with the surrounding cluster gas. These processes help shape the environment of the Phoenix Cluster and influence the evolution of nearby galaxies.
Scientific Importance:
The Phoenix A Black Hole is important because it may represent the upper limit of black hole growth in the observable universe. Understanding how such an enormous object formed remains one of the biggest challenges in modern astrophysics.
Traditional models suggest that black holes grow through mergers and the accumulation of surrounding matter. However, reaching masses approaching 100 billion Suns requires growth processes operating on scales rarely observed elsewhere in the universe.
The object has also become an important candidate for a proposed category known as Stupendously Large Black Holes (SLABs). These hypothetical black holes are so massive that they may require entirely new explanations for their formation and evolution.
By studying Phoenix A, astronomers hope to better understand how black holes regulate galaxy formation, influence star production, and shape the largest structures in the cosmos.
Observations & Modern Research:
One of the most unusual characteristics of the Phoenix Cluster is its enormous reservoir of hot gas. Observations indicate that this gas is cooling at a rate of approximately 3,820 solar masses per year, the highest cooling rate ever recorded in a galaxy cluster.
At the same time, Phoenix A is converting gas into new stars at an extraordinary rate of approximately 740 solar masses annually. This is hundreds of times greater than the current star formation rate of the Milky Way and represents one of the most intense starburst events known in the nearby universe.
In 2025, the James Webb Space Telescope provided a major breakthrough by detecting an intermediate-temperature gas component within the cluster. This discovery revealed a previously missing stage between extremely hot gas and much cooler gas, helping scientists understand how star formation is sustained at such remarkable levels.
Researchers continue to monitor the Phoenix Cluster using X-ray, infrared, radio, and optical observatories. Future observations may provide the first direct measurements of the black hole's mass and determine whether it truly deserves the title of the largest black hole known.

Gas Cooling Rate: ~3,820 Solar Masses per Year

name: Holmberg 15A Black Hole
Location: Center of Holmberg 15A Galaxy, Abell 85 Galaxy Cluster
Overview:
The Holmberg 15A Black Hole is one of the largest and most massive black holes ever discovered. It resides at the center of Holmberg 15A, a giant elliptical galaxy that dominates the Abell 85 galaxy cluster in the constellation Cetus. This enormous black hole has an estimated mass of approximately 40 billion times that of the Sun, placing it among the most extreme objects known in the observable universe.
Holmberg 15A itself is a colossal galaxy containing trillions of stars and vast amounts of dark matter. As the brightest galaxy within the Abell 85 cluster, it plays a central role in the gravitational dynamics of hundreds of neighboring galaxies. At its heart lies a black hole so massive that it challenges current theories regarding black hole formation and growth.
The black hole gained significant scientific attention in 2019 when researchers presented evidence supporting a mass of around 40 billion solar masses. Although several earlier studies proposed even larger estimates, the 2019 measurement remains one of the most reliable calculations available. Regardless of the exact value, the object firmly belongs to the rare class of ultramassive black holes and ranks among the largest ever identified.
Discovery & History:
Holmberg 15A was discovered around 1937 by Swedish astronomer Erik Holmberg, who conducted extensive studies of galaxies and galaxy clusters. For decades, the galaxy was primarily known as the brightest member of the Abell 85 cluster.
The galaxy became particularly famous in 2014 when astronomers reported that it appeared to possess the largest galactic core ever observed. Early measurements suggested that its central region spanned approximately 15,000 light-years. This unusual feature led researchers to suspect that repeated galaxy mergers may have dramatically reshaped the galaxy's center over billions of years.
Subsequent studies questioned whether the giant core actually existed, leading to scientific debate about the true structure of Holmberg 15A. Despite this disagreement, researchers continued investigating the galaxy and eventually focused on its central black hole.
A major breakthrough came in 2019 when a team led by K. Mehrgan used advanced dynamical modeling to estimate the black hole's mass at roughly 40 billion solar masses. This result immediately placed Holmberg 15A among the most significant black hole discoveries of the modern era.
Physical Characteristics:
The central black hole of Holmberg 15A has an estimated mass of approximately 40 billion Suns, making it nearly 10,000 times more massive than Sagittarius A*, the black hole at the center of the Milky Way.
Several earlier studies suggested even larger masses. Some estimates ranged from 150 billion to over 300 billion solar masses, although these values remain highly uncertain because they were derived using indirect methods rather than direct measurements. Even the more conservative estimate of 40 billion solar masses places Holmberg 15A among the largest confirmed black holes known.
The host galaxy itself is enormous. Holmberg 15A spans nearly 400,000 light-years across, making it several times larger than the Milky Way. It contains trillions of stars and possesses a total mass estimated at around 70 trillion solar masses when dark matter is included.
The black hole dominates the galaxy's central region through its immense gravitational influence, affecting the motion of stars and shaping the structure of the galaxy's core.
Scientific Importance:
Holmberg 15A provides astronomers with a valuable opportunity to study how ultramassive black holes form and evolve. Most black holes grow through mergers with other black holes and by accreting surrounding matter, but explaining the formation of objects this massive remains a major challenge.
Researchers believe Holmberg 15A likely underwent numerous galactic mergers throughout its history. Each merger may have contributed additional stars, gas, dark matter, and central black holes, gradually building the enormous object observed today.
The black hole also helps scientists investigate the relationship between galaxies and their central black holes. Observations suggest that the growth of a galaxy and the growth of its black hole are closely connected. Studying extreme examples such as Holmberg 15A allows researchers to test these theories under the most extreme conditions.
Because of its extraordinary size, Holmberg 15A is frequently used as a benchmark when comparing the largest black holes in the universe, alongside famous objects such as TON 618, Phoenix A, and M87*.
Observations & Modern Research:
Modern observations of Holmberg 15A have relied on a combination of optical telescopes, X-ray observatories, and advanced computational models. Researchers analyze the movement of stars near the galactic center to estimate the black hole's mass and determine how strongly it influences its surroundings.
The 2019 study that estimated a mass of 40 billion solar masses used detailed dynamical modeling of stellar motions within the galaxy. This approach provided one of the strongest pieces of evidence for the existence of such an enormous black hole.
Scientists continue to monitor Holmberg 15A because it may offer clues about the upper limits of black hole growth. Understanding how black holes reach tens of billions of solar masses remains one of the biggest questions in astrophysics.
Future observatories and improved measurement techniques may eventually provide even more precise estimates of the black hole's mass and reveal whether some of the larger historical estimates were accurate.

name: NGC 1277 Black Hole
Location: Center of NGC 1277 Galaxy, Perseus Cluster
Overview:
The NGC 1277 Black Hole is one of the most fascinating and controversial black holes ever studied. Located at the center of the compact lenticular galaxy NGC 1277 in the Perseus Cluster, this black hole gained worldwide attention after initial studies suggested that it was one of the most massive black holes ever discovered relative to the size of its host galaxy.
What makes NGC 1277 particularly unusual is not just the black hole itself, but the galaxy surrounding it. NGC 1277 is often described as a "relic galaxy" — a cosmic fossil that has remained largely unchanged since the early universe. Most of its stars formed approximately 12 billion years ago during a brief but intense period of star formation, after which the galaxy experienced very little further growth.
The black hole sits within a galaxy that appears to have avoided many of the mergers and interactions that shaped other large galaxies. Because of this, NGC 1277 offers astronomers a rare opportunity to study conditions that may resemble those of the early universe. The relationship between the galaxy and its central black hole continues to challenge traditional theories about how galaxies and black holes evolve together.
Discovery & History:
NGC 1277 was discovered on December 4, 1875, by Lawrence Parsons, the 4th Earl of Rosse. For over a century it remained simply another member of the Perseus Cluster, one of the most massive galaxy clusters in the nearby universe.
The galaxy became internationally famous in 2012 when astronomers using the Hobby–Eberly Telescope proposed that its central black hole possessed a mass of approximately 17 billion solar masses. At the time, this would have made it one of the largest known black holes and one of the most extreme examples of a black hole dominating its host galaxy.
This claim generated significant excitement within the astronomical community because the estimated black hole represented nearly 14% of the galaxy's total stellar mass. In most galaxies, black holes account for only a tiny fraction of the total mass.
However, later studies using more advanced instruments and higher-resolution observations challenged the original estimate. Over the following years, astronomers refined their measurements and concluded that the black hole was likely smaller than initially believed. Despite this revision, the object remains one of the most scientifically important black holes ever studied.
Physical Characteristics:
Current research suggests that the black hole's mass lies somewhere between 1.2 and 5 billion solar masses, with several studies favoring values near 4.9 billion solar masses. Although lower than the original estimate of 17 billion solar masses, this still places the object among the largest known supermassive black holes.
The host galaxy NGC 1277 is relatively small compared to giant elliptical galaxies such as M87 or Holmberg 15A. It spans roughly 52,700 light-years across, approximately half the diameter of the Milky Way. Despite its compact size, it contains an unusually dense concentration of stars packed into a small volume.
One of the most remarkable characteristics of NGC 1277 is its apparent lack of dark matter. Observations of the galaxy's rotation suggest that it contains significantly less dark matter than expected. This unusual property has made it an important target for researchers investigating alternative models of galaxy formation and evolution.
The galaxy also contains predominantly old, metal-rich stars that formed during an intense burst of star formation billions of years ago, further supporting the idea that it is a relic from the early universe.
Scientific Importance:
NGC 1277 is considered one of the best candidates for a relic galaxy. Such galaxies are thought to be surviving remnants of the first generation of massive galaxies that formed shortly after the universe's early growth phase.
Because the galaxy appears to have experienced relatively little change over billions of years, it provides astronomers with a unique window into the past. Studying NGC 1277 helps researchers understand how galaxies looked and behaved when the universe was much younger.
The black hole itself is equally important. The original estimate suggesting a mass of 17 billion solar masses sparked major discussions about whether black holes can grow faster than their host galaxies. Even though later measurements reduced the estimated mass, the system remains an important case study for understanding the connection between galaxy evolution and black hole growth.
The galaxy's unusually low dark matter content has also made it a valuable laboratory for testing models of cosmic structure formation and the role dark matter plays in shaping galaxies.
Observations & Modern Research:
Modern observations of NGC 1277 have been conducted using some of the world's most powerful telescopes, including the Hubble Space Telescope, Gemini Observatory, Keck Observatory, and the Hobby–Eberly Telescope.
Researchers have used adaptive optics and advanced spectroscopic techniques to measure the movement of stars near the galactic center. These observations have allowed scientists to refine estimates of the black hole's mass and better understand the galaxy's structure.
One of the most significant discoveries came from Hubble observations that revealed a surprising lack of metal-poor globular clusters around NGC 1277. Most galaxies contain both old and newly acquired globular clusters, but NGC 1277 appears to possess only a single population of ancient clusters. This finding strongly supports the idea that the galaxy has experienced very little growth since its formation.
Scientists continue to study NGC 1277 because it may represent one of the closest surviving examples of the compact galaxies that dominated the early universe. Future observations may reveal more about its black hole, dark matter content, and unusual evolutionary history.

Type: Supermassive Black Hole
Estimated Mass: ~1.2–5 Billion Solar Masses
Original Mass Estimate: ~17 Billion Solar Masses
Host Galaxy: NGC 1277
Galaxy Type: Lenticular Galaxy
Distance from Earth: ~220–240 Million Light-Years
Galaxy Cluster: Perseus Cluster
Constellation: Perseus
Discovery of Galaxy: 1875
Notable Feature: Candidate Relic Galaxy
Dark Matter Content: Unusually Low
Scientific Significance: Helps Study Early Galaxy Formation and Black Hole Evolution

name: OJ 287
Overview:
OJ 287 is one of the most extraordinary black hole systems ever discovered. Located roughly 4 billion light-years from Earth, it is classified as a blazar, a type of active galaxy whose powerful jet is directed almost toward our planet. This orientation makes OJ 287 appear exceptionally bright and allows astronomers to study some of the most energetic processes occurring in the universe.
Unlike most known supermassive black holes, OJ 287 is believed to contain not one but two supermassive black holes orbiting each other. The larger primary black hole has an estimated mass of approximately 18.35 billion solar masses, while the secondary black hole possesses a mass of roughly 150 million solar masses. Together, they form one of the best-known binary supermassive black hole systems ever observed.
The system is famous for producing periodic bursts of light that have been recorded for more than 130 years. These dramatic outbursts occur when the smaller black hole passes through the accretion disk of the larger black hole, generating enormous flashes of energy visible across billions of light-years. The unique behavior of OJ 287 has made it one of the most studied black hole systems in modern astronomy.
Discovery & History:
OJ 287 has been visible in astronomical records since at least 1887 and has been continuously monitored for over a century. Historical photographic plates revealed that the object undergoes powerful periodic brightenings approximately every 11 to 12 years.
For decades, astronomers struggled to explain these recurring outbursts. Various theories were proposed, but none fully accounted for the timing and intensity of the observed events. As more data accumulated, researchers began to suspect that the object might contain two supermassive black holes instead of one.
The binary black hole model eventually became the leading explanation. According to this theory, the smaller black hole periodically plunges through the accretion disk surrounding the larger black hole. Each impact releases an enormous burst of energy, producing the characteristic double-peaked flare pattern observed in the light curve.
Today, OJ 287 is regarded as one of the strongest pieces of evidence for the existence of a binary supermassive black hole system and continues to serve as a natural laboratory for studying black hole interactions.
Physical Characteristics:
The primary black hole in OJ 287 is estimated to contain approximately 18.35 billion times the mass of the Sun, placing it among the most massive black holes known. The secondary black hole is much smaller but still contains roughly 150 million solar masses, making it larger than many supermassive black holes found at the centers of galaxies.
The secondary black hole follows a highly elliptical orbit around the primary, completing one orbit approximately every 12 years. At its closest approach, it travels through the primary black hole's accretion disk, creating enormous bursts of light and radiation.
The system emits energy equivalent to more than one trillion Suns, making it brighter than the entire Milky Way Galaxy. Powerful relativistic jets launched from the vicinity of the primary black hole travel at speeds close to the speed of light and contribute to the object's classification as a blazar.
Researchers have also estimated that the primary black hole rotates at approximately 38% of the maximum theoretical spin allowed by Einstein's theory of relativity.
Scientific Importance:
OJ 287 is considered one of the most important black hole systems in astrophysics because it provides direct evidence for the existence of binary supermassive black holes. Such systems are expected to form when galaxies merge, bringing together the massive black holes located at their centers.
The system has also become a valuable testing ground for Einstein's General Theory of Relativity. By precisely tracking the orbit of the secondary black hole and predicting future outbursts, scientists have been able to test relativistic effects under extreme gravitational conditions.
The predictable nature of OJ 287's outbursts has allowed researchers to verify theoretical models with remarkable accuracy. Each successful prediction strengthens confidence in our understanding of gravity, black holes, and orbital mechanics.
In addition, OJ 287 offers insight into the future evolution of galaxies and black holes. The system provides a rare opportunity to observe a binary black hole pair during the final stages before eventual merger.
Observations & Modern Research:
Modern observations of OJ 287 involve telescopes operating across the electromagnetic spectrum, including radio, optical, infrared, X-ray, and gamma-ray observatories. Space-based missions such as Spitzer and Swift have contributed significantly to understanding the system's behavior.
One of the most impressive achievements involving OJ 287 has been the successful prediction of its periodic outbursts. By applying complex orbital models based on General Relativity, astronomers have accurately forecasted several major flares years before they occurred.
In 2020, observations from the Spitzer Space Telescope helped refine measurements of the binary orbit and improve estimates of the masses of both black holes. These findings strengthened the binary black hole interpretation and provided new information about the dynamics of the system.
Researchers have also determined that the orbit of the smaller black hole is gradually shrinking due to the emission of gravitational waves. As the system loses energy, the two black holes spiral closer together. Current estimates suggest that the pair will eventually merge in approximately 10,000 years, producing an enormous gravitational wave event.
OJ 287 remains a target for future observations by next-generation observatories and is considered a potential candidate for direct imaging by instruments such as the Event Horizon Telescope.
Quick Stats:
Type: Binary Supermassive Black Hole System (Blazar)
Primary Black Hole Mass: ~18.35 Billion Solar Masses
Secondary Black Hole Mass: ~150 Million Solar Masses
Distance from Earth: ~4 Billion Light-Years
Constellation: Cancer
Orbital Period: ~12 Years
Redshift: 0.306
Luminosity: Over 1 Trillion Times the Sun
Notable Feature: Periodic Double-Peaked Outbursts
Scientific Significance: Strongest Known Candidate for a Binary Supermassive Black Hole System
Future Event: Expected Black Hole Merger in ~10,000 Years
Major Observatories: Spitzer, Swift, VLBA, Event Horizon Telescope Candidates

name: NGC 4889 Black Hole
Location: Center of NGC 4889 Galaxy, Coma Cluster
Overview:
The NGC 4889 Black Hole is one of the largest known black holes in the nearby universe and ranks among the most massive black holes ever directly measured. Located at the center of NGC 4889, a giant elliptical galaxy in the Coma Cluster, this enormous object possesses an estimated mass of approximately 21 billion times that of the Sun.
NGC 4889 itself is an extraordinary galaxy. It is one of the brightest and most massive galaxies within the Coma Cluster and serves as a dominant gravitational force within the region. The galaxy contains trillions of stars, an enormous halo extending more than a million light-years across, and one of the largest known populations of globular clusters. At its center lies a black hole whose gravitational influence shapes the evolution of both the galaxy and its surrounding environment.
The black hole is believed to have accumulated its immense mass through billions of years of galaxy mergers and matter accretion. Today, it remains relatively quiet compared to active quasars, but evidence suggests that it was once an extremely energetic object capable of producing powerful emissions throughout the cluster.
Discovery & History:
NGC 4889 was first observed in 1785 by the famous astronomer William Herschel during his survey of deep-sky objects. At the time, it appeared as a faint nebulous patch through early telescopes and was later included in astronomical catalogs as one of the brightest members of the Coma Cluster.
For centuries, astronomers studied the galaxy primarily because of its enormous size and importance within the Coma Cluster. However, its central black hole remained hidden until modern observational techniques allowed researchers to analyze the motion of stars near the galactic core.
A major breakthrough occurred in 2011 when astronomers measured the velocity of stars surrounding the galactic center. Their observations revealed the presence of an ultramassive black hole containing approximately 21 billion solar masses. This discovery immediately placed NGC 4889 among a select group of galaxies hosting some of the largest black holes ever measured.
The finding also demonstrated that giant elliptical galaxies can harbor black holes far larger than those found in spiral galaxies such as the Milky Way, providing important clues about how massive galaxies evolve over cosmic time.
Physical Characteristics:
The central black hole of NGC 4889 is estimated to contain around 21 billion solar masses, making it roughly 5,200 times more massive than Sagittarius A*, the black hole at the center of the Milky Way.
Studies suggest that the black hole's mass could lie anywhere between 6 billion and 37 billion solar masses, but the best current estimate remains approximately 21 billion solar masses. Even at the lower end of this range, it remains one of the most massive black holes ever identified.
The event horizon is enormous. Depending on the mass estimate used, its diameter may range from approximately 20 billion to 124 billion kilometers. At its largest estimate, the event horizon would be more than twelve times wider than Pluto's orbit around the Sun.
The host galaxy is equally impressive. NGC 4889 spans nearly 300,000 light-years and possesses an extended stellar halo exceeding one million light-years in diameter. The galaxy is estimated to contain trillions of stars and may possess a total mass of up to 15 trillion solar masses when dark matter is included.
Scientific Importance:
NGC 4889 is one of the most important galaxies for studying the upper limits of black hole growth. Its enormous black hole provides valuable insight into how supermassive black holes evolve over billions of years through mergers and accretion.
Researchers use NGC 4889 as a benchmark when comparing the largest black holes in the universe. Alongside objects such as TON 618, Phoenix A, Holmberg 15A, and M87*, it helps define the extreme end of black hole evolution.
The galaxy also serves as a model for understanding giant elliptical galaxies. Unlike spiral galaxies that continue forming new stars, galaxies such as NGC 4889 are dominated by old stellar populations and contain relatively little cold gas for future star formation.
Studying NGC 4889 helps astronomers understand how galaxy clusters evolve, how black holes influence their environments, and how the largest structures in the universe develop over time.
Observations & Modern Research:
Modern observations of NGC 4889 utilize optical telescopes, X-ray observatories, radio telescopes, and space-based instruments. Researchers study the movement of stars near the galactic center to estimate the black hole's mass and investigate its influence on surrounding matter.
The galaxy is a strong source of X-ray, ultraviolet, and radio radiation. Observations indicate that the central black hole heats surrounding gas through energetic interactions involving infalling matter. This heating helps regulate conditions within the galaxy and contributes to the dynamics of the surrounding cluster environment.
Astronomers have also discovered that NGC 4889 hosts approximately 15,800 globular clusters, making it one of the richest globular cluster systems known. These ancient star clusters provide valuable information about the galaxy's formation history and merger events.
Evidence suggests that NGC 4889 may once have functioned as a quasar. The ionized gas detected around the central black hole indicates that it likely experienced a highly active phase in the distant past before exhausting most of the easily available material needed to fuel continued activity.
Today, researchers continue monitoring the galaxy to refine measurements of the black hole's mass and better understand the relationship between giant galaxies and their ultramassive black holes.

name: Abell 1201 BCG Black Hole
Location: Center of Abell 1201 Brightest Cluster Galaxy (BCG), Abell 1201 Galaxy Cluster
Overview:
The Abell 1201 BCG Black Hole is one of the most intriguing and scientifically important black hole candidates ever discovered. Located at the center of the Brightest Cluster Galaxy (BCG) in the Abell 1201 galaxy cluster, this enormous object has attracted worldwide attention because it may contain more than 30 billion times the mass of the Sun.
Unlike most ultramassive black holes, which are detected through stellar motion or gas dynamics, the black hole in Abell 1201 was identified using an entirely different technique: gravitational lensing. This phenomenon occurs when a massive object bends and magnifies light from a more distant galaxy located behind it. The distortion of this background light provided astronomers with evidence that an enormous concentration of unseen mass exists at the galaxy's center.
The discovery has made Abell 1201 one of the most valuable systems for studying both black hole growth and dark matter. The galaxy serves as a natural cosmic laboratory where researchers can investigate how gravity behaves on enormous scales and how the largest black holes in the universe form and evolve.
Discovery & History:
The galaxy first gained scientific interest because it acts as a powerful gravitational lens. Astronomers observed a distinctive arc of light near the galaxy, created when light from a more distant background galaxy was bent by the galaxy's immense gravity.
In 2003, researchers began studying the unusual lensing effect in greater detail. The curved arc provided evidence that the galaxy contained an enormous amount of mass concentrated near its center. Initial studies focused primarily on understanding the distribution of dark matter within the galaxy cluster.
Over time, astronomers developed increasingly sophisticated lens models to explain the observed structure. A major breakthrough occurred when researchers discovered a faint counterimage associated with the gravitational arc. This feature was difficult to explain using conventional models and suggested the presence of a massive central object.
In 2023, a detailed analysis using high-resolution observations strongly supported the existence of an ultramassive black hole at the galaxy's center. The results indicated that the black hole's mass may be approximately 32.7 billion solar masses, making it one of the largest black holes ever detected.
Physical Characteristics:
The black hole at the center of Abell 1201 BCG is estimated to possess a mass of approximately 32.7 billion Suns. Due to observational uncertainties, the actual value may range from approximately 11.5 billion to 53.9 billion solar masses.
If the higher estimates prove correct, the object would rank among the largest black holes ever discovered, comparable to famous ultramassive black holes such as TON 618, Phoenix A, and Holmberg 15A.
The host galaxy itself is enormous. Classified as a giant elliptical galaxy, it spans nearly 485,000 light-years across and dominates the central region of the Abell 1201 galaxy cluster. The galaxy contains hundreds of billions of stars and serves as the gravitational center of its surrounding environment.
One unusual characteristic of the system is that the galaxy is not perfectly spherical. Its stellar distribution is significantly elongated, which complicates efforts to accurately model the black hole's mass and the surrounding dark matter distribution.
Scientific Importance:
The Abell 1201 Black Hole is historically important because it may represent the first ultramassive black hole whose mass was measured primarily through gravitational lensing techniques.
Traditionally, astronomers estimate black hole masses by studying the movement of stars and gas near the galactic center. In Abell 1201, however, researchers used the bending of light from a distant background galaxy to infer the presence of an enormous central mass.
This achievement demonstrates that gravitational lensing can be used as a powerful tool for detecting black holes in distant galaxies, potentially opening a new pathway for discovering ultramassive black holes across the universe.
The system also presents an important challenge to current theories of dark matter. Some models suggest the galaxy contains a relatively shallow dark matter distribution, while others require a more concentrated dark matter halo. Understanding which explanation is correct could significantly improve our knowledge of the invisible matter that dominates much of the universe.
Observations & Modern Research:
Abell 1201 remains an active area of research because it sits at the intersection of two major fields of astrophysics: black hole physics and dark matter studies.
Observations have revealed a striking gravitational arc created by a background galaxy located far behind the cluster. Detailed analysis of this arc and its faint counterimage has provided some of the strongest evidence for the presence of a massive central black hole.
Researchers continue to debate the exact relationship between the black hole and the surrounding dark matter halo. Some models suggest that a larger black hole requires a steeper dark matter concentration, while smaller black hole solutions imply a more diffuse dark matter distribution.
The 2023 follow-up study significantly strengthened the case for an ultramassive black hole and revised its estimated mass upward. If future observations confirm these findings, Abell 1201 will become one of the most important examples of gravitational lensing being used to directly measure a black hole's mass.
Future observations using advanced telescopes may provide even more precise measurements and help determine whether this system requires modifications to current theories of black hole growth, dark matter behavior, or both.
